title: 2025 10 15 - HE
displayTitle: 2025-10-14 - Health Committee - Petition of Gareth Lowndes: Increase resources for the Health and Disability Commissioner
topic: Petition of Gareth Lowndes: Increase resources for the Health and Disability Commissioner
description: 15 October 2025 | Health Committee Petition of Gareth Lowndes: Increase resources for the Health and Disability Commissioner Office of the Health and Disability Commissioner Morag McDowell, Health and Disability Commissioner Dr Vanessa Caldwell, Deputy Commissioner, Health and Disability
descriptionSummary: Petition of Gareth Lowndes: Increase resources for the Health and Disability Commissioner. Office of the Health and Disability Commissioner. Morag McDowell, Health and Disability Commissioner

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. Good morning. Good to have you here. We have the Office of the Health and Disability. I'm here for 20 minutes. We're in public session. I will hand it over to you. You Thank you. I want to start by acknowledging and thanking Mr.

Lowndes for his petition. I had the privilege of meeting with Mr. Lowndes to understand his experience of HDC processes, and I have no hesitation in extending to him a further apology for the delays in managing his complaint and the impact that those delays had on both him and his family.

I am very aware more generally of negative impact that UE delay and the assessment and investigation of complaints can have on participants to the process. I want to reassure Mr. Lowndes and this committee that HDC is fully committed to reducing delays in our process and improving communication with parties to the complaint.

I also believe that if Mr. Lowndes were to make his complaint today, his experience would be very different owing to new processes that we have in place. I'd like to start by providing some context to the committee, uh, including our recent performance indicators. And I'd be certainly happy to provide this in writing to the committee if it would assist.

Mr. Low's complaint was made in January, 2022 at a time when HDC was managing an unprecedented and unanticipated increase in the volume of complaints owing to the pandemic. Indeed, in the 2122 year HDC received a 25% increase in its volume of complaints.

This together with the novel and complex issues that we were having to manage as part of the COVID pandemic, undoubtedly put us under significant pressure. Furthermore, rather than declining after the pandemic complaints, volumes have remained high. I'd like to give you a snapshot of recent funding.

HDCs current baseline is 17.8 million, which includes 2.1 million of funding designated to the aged care function Aged Care commissioner function in the 2021 budget. HDC was given a time limited funding increase of 2.9 million, which was tagged to sustainability and new functions under the end of Life Choice Act.

That funding was due to expire in June, 2024. However, uh, government extended that funding to 30 June, 2025 and this year, HDC was granted one-off funds of 1 million targeted to help us reduce the backdrop, meaning that our baseline this year was reduced by 1.9 million instead of the anticipated 2.9 million.

And this has necessitated a restructure within HDC, including a reduction of FTE effective from four August. However, notwithstanding these funding challenges and sustained compliance volume, HDCs performance has significantly improved over the past 18 months, owing to a small re leadership restructure in 2024.

And staffing process and operational changes directed at streamlining processes and ensuring that our valuable resources are directed to those complaints, uh, that need our involvement, our program of system improvement is yielding very positive and consistent results to the extent that we are making significant gains in reducing ca our cases on hand, which will in turn allow us to address delays in our process.

We believe that we can maintain our complaints resolution function within our current baseline. Although gaining a business as usual complaint level will take some time. And I think the recent figures speak for themselves in 24 25 HDC increased our number of closures by 40% and reduced our number of open complaints by 33%.

We are now closing more complaints than we receive with a net closure rate about 120%, 120%. This puts us in a much better position to manage our backlog and timeliness issues. We have made progress in significantly reducing the backlog in parts of the business.

For example, in our complaints assessment team, which um, manages about 92% of our complaints, we have reduced the number of complaints on hand by 47% in 24 25. And approximately 70% of complaints received by HDC are closed within six months.

However, it is fair to say that we are still grappling with the flow on effect of the 2022 COVID surge, particularly in our investigation space. Space, which deals with the more serious and complex complaints and which require more resource to resolve. Timeliness is still a problem in this area with investigations taking on average at the moment, 2.7 years to complete.

However, we have a backlog plan in place and we are aiming to reach a business as usual state by September, 2026 by business as usual. What I mean is a, um, a, an ideal number of cases on hand within that team, ideally in our view, noting both the process of investigation and that that quasi-judicial in nature.

We think that investigations should take between 18 to 24 months to complete. It is important to recognize that not all delay rests at the feet of H-D-C-H-D-C is reliant on external parties to provide information to it and an pressured health and disability system.

Time extensions are often requested. Investigations are also in-depth pro uh, in depth independent processes, often involving analysis of voluminous quantities of information and requiring very careful decision making. They're processes that are taken very seriously, not just by HDC, but also by the providers of service, noting that it's through the formal process of investigation that breaches of the code of rights are determined by us.

Might jump in shortly Yes. Because we've got 20 minutes. Yes. I phone For seven. Um, and I want members to have a good shot Of questions. I sure. Can I just, um, make another few points then Mr. Chair? Um, do a Couple more minutes and then I'll open. Yeah. I did want to, um, reference that um, we do have very robust processes in place, not withstanding those issues, to escalate issues of public safety and quality, um, improvement that as, as they come to hand.

I also wanted to alert the committee to our IT capacity, which I'm pleased to report. We have received $500,000 from government, uh, in June, 2025. This was a project we had already committed to and made savings for. We are in the build phase of that project and we expect to, uh, have that new complaints management system in place by March, 2026. Alright, I'm gonna, I'm gonna open up to Chris and just, just conscious of time.

Yes. Um, and I see a few hands up. We'll start with Jenny and then I've got Asha. Thank you very much for, uh, being in front of committee today. Appreciate that you are here. Just a couple of questions around, um, at, at the beginning of your comment, you talked about you'd had a 25% increase in 2022.

Correct. Post COVID. Can you tell us what, what does 25% represent in terms of numbers? And you also mentioned that, uh, these are a, a nature of serious and complex complaints. Um, in terms of the stuff that's come post COVID, can you sort of just extrapolate that out a little bit? So in the 20 21, 20 20 to 2021 year, we received 2,721 complaints and 2122 3,413.

And those are not just, we didn't receive just additional complaints. We also received, we received inquiries as well. And of course our advocacy service also receives complaints. This is just HDC I'm talking about. Alright. And so the nature of, uh, of serious and complex complaints Yeah. For invest COVID, Uh, for investigating for investigations or in in the COVID time, Uh, for investigating as well as, yeah.

When complaints come into the HDC, one of the very first things we do is we assess the seriousness of the complaint and it, we, we receive a very broad range of complaints from those of perhaps, um, lesser seriousness, which we can refer straight back to the provider or to our advocacy service to manage at source. The more serious complaints often involve very serious outcomes, including serious, um, serious health outcomes as, as well as death, but also serious systemic issues and or, um, serious failings in the provision of care.

And we measure that against the Code of Rights, of course, which involves a suite of informed consent rights, uh, reasonable care rights, right. To support, um, et cetera. So that, and, and they can be extremely serious. So just to up on that and how, how many of those of a serious nature are as a result of COVID Are as a result of COVID in that COVID period?

Yeah. Um, actually not, not too many at the moment, but it's the fact that we received that enormous surge in that year, which essentially we had to manage, increase in volume coming in the front door, the various, um, complex and quite unusual issues that we were having to manage during the COVID time.

And that had flow on effects for the rest of the system. And that complaint volume was maintained. There was no significant drop following the COVID pandemic. Alright. And good. Um, thank you very much. I just wanna pick up on, um, what I think is the tone of the petition, which is that there are big systems changes that can happen at the end of a lengthy kind of inquiry process, but for many people this is about preventing, uh, a similar experience for other patients.

You have mentioned third parties and waiting on information. Is that after the, um, complaints have been lodged or are you taking into account before that as well? What's the story there? Yeah, so we have a memorandum of understanding with the coroner's court and we have a conversation right at the start of the complaint as to which, um, which process will take precedence. So sometimes the coroner goes first, sometimes the HDC goes first. I was actually talking about, um, the gathering of information during the HDC process.

So from the point at which the complaint is lodged to the conclusion of the complaint. Um, and as I said, we are reliant on those external parties to provide us the information. And during that time, you have the ability if you are concerned about public safety right. To anybody including a minister. Absolutely.

Um, what, what course of action would you expect a minister to take if they received one of your letters? Um, so we, we have written to the minister or certainly to other agencies as well, and we monitor the response to those concerns when they're raised. If we are not satisfied with the response, we will respond again and, and ask for the issue to be addressed.

And I, as I said, I want to reassure the committee that we act swiftly on any public safety concerns that we see and quality improvement measures, and we continue that work even while the investigation and assessment is taking place. Is that,

Uh, changes? I mean, it would be, um, uh, undesirable if you reduced your waiting time by, um, raising your threshold for what you investigated. Um, in 2020 when I first became commissioner, there were concerns raised by the ombudsman at that time that the threshold for investigation was too high and we have consciously reduced that threshold.

So the number of investigations has gone from roughly four to 5% to seven to 8% of complaints that go through to investigation. I feel that we have got, um, the threshold correct. The way we, that we can assess that is by assessing the breaches that we're determining the number of breaches.

Uh, and we are also very much focused on the resolution needs of the consumer. So we try and very much to engage with the consumer to understand, uh, what would meet their needs. But we also take into account public interest. So if there are, and we have broad powers as, as I've alluded to, um, we can raise concerns.

Quick point of clarification, different to, um, just proceeding down an investigation route if, if we need to. The point about the breaches, I assume if you are investigating um, uh, too many complaints, they would be diluted and you'd be less likely to find a breach in the set of, um, yes.

Uh, cases that you investigate. Is that quantified as a number and reported in your annual reports? Uh, yes it does. Thank You. You've got a so don't you. Yes. Thank you. I, I see this petitioner, um, was waiting three years. How many cases, what's the number of cases which are three years or older? That's cent. Uh, I don't actually have the number of, um, files in investigations that we have that are over three years old. Uh, but uh, it is um, approximately, uh, 40% of the, um, investigation files that we currently have.

Um, we do, um, uh, and we have has Morag alluded to, um, have a, um, a, a structured plan to, um, prioritize those older files to get through, um, to completion. There are various reasons why many of them are delayed. So you, you're saying you're calling them investigations.

Is that a complaint that has had some action on it versus a complaint that has been sitting there for three years? Yes. So, um, we have recently changed our processes such that we are identifying and getting complaints more quickly through to the investigations space.

Um, investigations has have to meet a legal threshold or legal tests that's in the legislation. Sometimes we gather information prior to that process and our complaints assessment phase and then we evaluate that information to determine whether the statutory test has been met for, for progression through to the investigations we are counting, delay from the point of complaint through to completion, be that in the complaints assessment team or our investigation starts.

There's definitely been work on them, but it's fair to say that there have been delays within that process. Absolutely. Uh, and I appreciate you talking about, uh, delays in information from other, other agencies. How, how many of, how much of that delay is sitting with the HDC and how much of that delay is sitting with external players?

I think that that is very difficult to quantify because it does very much depend on the individual complaint. I, what I can say is yes, of course there has been some delay as for the reasons that I've outlined, um, in HDC processes. But take for example, our need for expert advice.

When you have a complex clinical matter, we don't have in-house clinical advice available and we have to seek that externally. You have to identify the right expert advisor that can take time. 'cause we use a peer for peer situation. And uh, sorry, I'll jump in there.
